lessons.award: Padma Vibhushan, Padma Bhushan; Field Marshal (1973)
lessons.value: Meticulous preparation over political pressure — refused to rush to war
lessons.hook: When the Prime Minister of India ordered him to go to war, he said no — and that refusal won India its greatest victory.

blocks.title: Life Story
blocks.body:
Sam Hormusji Framji Jamshedji Manekshaw was born on 3 April 1914 in Amritsar to a Parsi family with roots in Valsad, Gujarat. His father, a doctor who had settled in Punjab, raised him with a sense of discipline and clear purpose. Commissioned from the Indian Military Academy, Dehradun, in 1934 into the Royal Scots Fusiliers and later affiliated to the Gorkha Rifles, Manekshaw quickly proved himself a soldier of exceptional competence and personal courage. In the Burma campaign of World War II, he was shot five times and left for dead on the battlefield — he survived, was awarded the Military Cross, and the British general who operated on him reportedly said that only a Pathan could have survived such wounds. 'I am not a Pathan,' Manekshaw replied, 'I am a Parsi.' It was his first recorded act of professional irreverence toward authority, a quality that would define him.

By the time of the 1971 crisis, Manekshaw was Chief of Army Staff. When the refugee crisis from East Pakistan erupted in April 1971 and Prime Minister Indira Gandhi demanded an immediate military response, Manekshaw attended a Cabinet meeting and said, bluntly, that the Army was not ready. He told the Prime Minister that if she ordered him to go to war that week, India would be defeated. He presented a list of what was needed — bridging equipment, artillery reserves, tank overhauling, accumulated logistics — and asked for six to eight months. Indira Gandhi, to her great credit, agreed. Manekshaw used every one of those months to prepare the most thorough military campaign India had ever mounted. He coordinated all three services, planned the simultaneous three-front strategy, and worked with the Mukti Bahini (Bangladesh freedom fighters) to pre-position intelligence and support inside East Pakistan.

The war began on 3 December 1971 and was over in thirteen days. The surrender of 93,000 Pakistani troops on 16 December 1971 was the most complete victory in South Asian military history. In 1973, in recognition of his outstanding service across multiple wars, Sam Manekshaw was promoted to the rank of Field Marshal — a five-star rank that had never before been awarded in independent India and has not been awarded since. He retired to Wellington in the Nilgiris and lived simply, remaining sharp and outspoken until the very end. He died on 27 June 2008, aged 94, saluted by the entire nation.

blocks.title: Impact on India
blocks.body: Manekshaw's insistence on proper preparation before the 1971 war is one of the most instructive episodes in modern Indian civil-military relations. By refusing to yield to political pressure and providing an honest professional assessment of the Army's readiness, he ensured that when India did fight, it fought with decisive superiority rather than improvised desperation. The result — a 13-day war, 93,000 prisoners, and the birth of a new nation — validated his approach completely. His leadership also demonstrated that a military commander's highest duty is not blind compliance but honest counsel: telling the political leadership what it needs to hear, not what it wants to hear. As the only Field Marshal in independent Indian history, he represents the pinnacle of what the Indian Army can produce.

blocks.title: Legacy
blocks.body: Sam Manekshaw is remembered as 'Sam Bahadur' — Brave Sam — a nickname given to him by his beloved Gorkha soldiers, and it became the title of a 2023 biographical film starring Vicky Kaushal. The Manekshaw Centre in New Delhi, the Army's premier conference facility, is named after him. He is quoted, studied, and celebrated for his wit as much as his generalship — perhaps most memorably for the line, 'If a man says he is not afraid of dying, he is either lying or he is a Gorkha.' His story is a lesson in the power of professional integrity, preparation over impulse, and the rare courage it takes to speak inconvenient truths to those in power.

lessons.reflection: Manekshaw told the Prime Minister he wasn't ready — a rare act of professional honesty under enormous pressure. What does it cost a leader to speak inconvenient truths to power? Have you ever had to tell someone in authority something they did not want to hear?

options: He said the Army was ready and could attack within a week
He said he was not ready and needed six to eight months to prepare properly
He said war was unnecessary and recommended a diplomatic solution
He said the Navy and Air Force needed to act first

quiz.question: What nickname did Manekshaw's Gorkha soldiers give him, which later became the title of a biographical film?
To what rank was Sam Manekshaw promoted in 1973, making him the first and only holder of that rank in independent India?
In which year was Sam Manekshaw promoted to the rank of Field Marshal?

lessons.value: Refused to bail out of a burning tank — fought until fatally hit
lessons.hook: His tank was on fire, his commanding officer ordered him out — and he refused. 'My tank is still operational, Sir.'

blocks.title: Life Story
blocks.body:
Arun Khetarpal was born on 14 October 1950 in Pune to Brigadier M.L. Khetarpal and his wife Indira — a military family in which service was both tradition and expectation. He was commissioned into the 17 Poona Horse, one of India's finest and oldest armoured regiments, on 13 June 1971, just six months before his death. He was 21 years old and had barely completed his basic officer training when the war broke out.

On 16 December 1971 — the very day Pakistan was signing the Instrument of Surrender in Dhaka — ferocious tank battles were still raging on the Western front in the Shakargarh salient, in the Punjab sector. Khetarpal's troop of tanks was engaged in intense combat at the Battle of Basantar, one of the largest tank battles of the entire war. When his Centurion tank was hit by Pakistani anti-tank fire and caught fire, his commanding officer ordered him to bail out immediately. Khetarpal's response, transmitted over the radio, has become one of the most quoted lines in the history of the Indian Army: 'My tank is still operational, Sir. I am not abandoning it.' He continued fighting, destroying several more Pakistani tanks, driving the attack forward. He was hit again — this time fatally — and died inside his tank. He had been an officer for exactly six months.

The Pakistani tank commander who faced him that day, who later became Brigadier Nisar Ahmad of the Pakistan Army, visited India years afterward. He paid public tribute to Khetarpal, saying he had never in his military career faced a braver or more determined soldier. It was an extraordinary tribute across the lines of two nations.

blocks.title: Impact on India
blocks.body: The Battle of Basantar was critical to securing India's gains on the Western front. The Shakargarh salient was the most important piece of Pakistani-held territory in the Punjab sector, and the armoured battle there — fought at the same time as the surrender in Dhaka — ensured that Pakistan had no remaining military option in the west either. Khetarpal's individual action kept momentum in the attack at a moment when it could have faltered. His Param Vir Chakra citation credits him with the destruction of at least four enemy tanks in his final engagement. He is the youngest officer to have received the PVC since Arun Khetarpal's own regiment mentor, Lieutenant Colonel A.B. Tarapore, who also won the PVC in the 1965 war with 17 Poona Horse.

blocks.title: Legacy
blocks.body: Second Lieutenant Arun Khetarpal is one of the most beloved figures in the 17 Poona Horse, where his portrait and the citation of his PVC occupy the regimental centre of honour. He is commemorated every year on 16 December — not only as Vijay Diwas but as the day the regiment's youngest officer died with his tank still fighting. His father, Brigadier M.L. Khetarpal, in a moment of extraordinary dignity, donated his son's awarded Param Vir Chakra to the Poona Horse regiment so that it would always remain with the men his son had commanded. His story appears in military training syllabi as the definitive example of the bond between a soldier and his duty — a bond that not even fire could break.

lessons.reflection: Arun Khetarpal had been an officer for only six months. He refused to bail out of a burning tank. What separates trained courage from natural fearlessness? Is there a moment in your life where duty and instinct pulled you in the same direction, no matter the cost?

quiz.question: In which regiment did Second Lieutenant Arun Khetarpal serve during the 1971 war?
What was the name of the battle in which Arun Khetarpal was killed on 16 December 1971?
What did Khetarpal say when his commanding officer ordered him to bail out of his burning tank?

'My tank is still operational, Sir. I am not abandoning it.'
'The enemy is 50 yards from us. I shall not withdraw.'
'Send reinforcements — we can still hold the line.'

Which Pakistani officer later visited India and publicly called Khetarpal the bravest soldier he had ever faced?

lessons.hero: Flying Officer Nirmal Jit Singh Sekhon
lessons.award: Param Vir Chakra (Posthumous) — only IAF recipient
lessons.value: Took off alone into impossible odds — the only IAF officer to earn the Param Vir Chakra
lessons.hook: Six enemy jets were attacking. Every other Indian aircraft was away. He was on standby — and he chose to take off alone.

blocks.title: Life Story
blocks.body:
Nirmal Jit Singh Sekhon was born on 17 July 1943 in Issewal village near Ludhiana, Punjab, into a Sikh farming family. He was commissioned into the Indian Air Force in 1967 and assigned to 18 Squadron, nicknamed 'Flying Bullets', which flew the Folland Gnat — a small, nimble, and formidably agile interceptor jet that IAF pilots had nicknamed 'Sabre Slayer' for its record against Pakistan's American-supplied F-86 Sabre fighters.

On 14 December 1971, two days before the final surrender, the Pakistan Air Force launched a major strike against Srinagar airfield using six North American F-86 Sabre jets in two waves. Most of 18 Squadron's aircraft were engaged on operations elsewhere in the valley. Sekhon was at the airfield on readiness duty — the pilot designated to scramble at short notice if needed. When the air raid warning sounded and six enemy jets were confirmed inbound, Sekhon did not hesitate. Despite being told the odds were impossible, he taxied his Gnat to the runway and took off alone.

What followed was one of the most unequal — and courageous — aerial engagements in Indian military history. Flying his Gnat against six Sabres, Sekhon manoeuvred aggressively, shot down one Sabre confirmed, and damaged at least one other before his own aircraft was hit. He was killed in the engagement. His body and his aircraft were never recovered from the mountain terrain surrounding the airfield. He was 28 years old and, by several accounts, engaged to be married.

He was posthumously awarded the Param Vir Chakra — the only officer of the Indian Air Force ever to receive it. His name is now synonymous, within the IAF, with the idea that courage is not the absence of awareness of the odds, but the decision to act in spite of them.

blocks.title: Impact on India
blocks.body: Sekhon's single-aircraft intercept disrupted the Pakistani strike on Srinagar airfield sufficiently to prevent it from achieving its objectives. In the context of the air war over Kashmir, the preservation of Srinagar airfield's operational capability was strategically significant — it remained usable throughout the final days of the war. His action also had a profound morale impact: the story of one pilot, alone against six, spread rapidly through the IAF and became emblematic of the spirit with which the service fought the entire 1971 campaign. More broadly, his story challenges civilians to think about what readiness means — not just the readiness of equipment, but the readiness of character.

blocks.title: Legacy
blocks.body: Flying Officer Nirmal Jit Singh Sekhon is honoured at the IAF base in Srinagar and at the Air Force Station Halwara near Ludhiana. A bust of Sekhon stands at the National War Memorial in New Delhi among the nation's highest gallantry awardees. 18 Squadron continues to fly, and his name is perpetuated as the gold standard of the squadron's fighting spirit. He is the only IAF officer ever awarded the Param Vir Chakra — in an air force that has produced some of the finest fighter pilots in the world, that distinction speaks to the singular nature of what he did on 14 December 1971.

lessons.reflection: Sekhon was on standby — he could have stayed grounded. Instead he took off alone into impossible odds. What inner voice compels someone to act when no one is watching and no one would blame them for staying? Have you ever acted when it would have been easier and safer not to?

quiz.question: In which IAF squadron did Flying Officer Nirmal Jit Singh Sekhon serve during the 1971 war?
Which aircraft did Flying Officer Sekhon fly in his lone intercept on 14 December 1971?
Which airfield did Flying Officer Sekhon defend alone against six Pakistani Sabre jets?
What is the gallantry award received posthumously by Flying Officer Sekhon, and what makes it historically unique?
options: Vir Chakra — the highest award given to an IAF pilot in the 1971 war
Maha Vir Chakra — the first ever awarded to an IAF officer
Param Vir Chakra — the only time it has ever been awarded to an IAF officer
Ashoka Chakra — the highest peacetime gallantry award

quiz.question: How old was Flying Officer Sekhon when he was killed in action on 14 December 1971?

lessons.value: Charged two bunkers alone with a bayonet — gave his life to save his company
lessons.hook: Wounded, under fire, with his company pinned down — he picked up his bayonet and charged not one enemy bunker, but two.

blocks.title: Life Story
blocks.body:
Albert Ekka was born on 27 December 1942 in Jari village, Gumla district, in what was then Bihar and is now Jharkhand. He was a member of the Oraon tribal community — one of India's ancient Adivasi peoples — and a Roman Catholic. He grew up in modest circumstances in the Chota Nagpur plateau, a region that has sent soldiers to the Indian Army for generations despite being one of the most economically marginalised parts of the country. Ekka enlisted in 14 Guards and was a Lance Naik — a junior non-commissioned officer — by 1971.

On 3 December 1971, the very day India entered the war, Ekka's battalion was ordered to assault the Pakistani stronghold at Gangasagar in present-day Bangladesh, on the Eastern front. The advance stalled almost immediately: a well-fortified Pakistani bunker opened heavy automatic fire, pinning down the entire company and causing casualties. With the advance stopped and men dying around him, Ekka acted without orders and without hesitation. He charged the bunker alone, silenced it with his bayonet — killing its occupants in close-quarters combat — and enabled the company to move forward.

As the company advanced, a second bunker opened fire. Already wounded from the first action, Ekka charged it too. He bayoneted the crew of the second bunker as well, but was hit mortally by fire as he did so. He died inside the second bunker, his body found alongside those of the enemy soldiers he had killed. He was 28 years old. The company he had saved went on to take its objective.

Albert Ekka was posthumously awarded the Param Vir Chakra. The Albert Ekka Chowk — a central traffic circle in Ranchi — bears his name today, ensuring that the capital of his home state remembers him.

blocks.title: Impact on India
blocks.body: Ekka's action at Gangasagar was decisive at the tactical level: his destruction of the first bunker broke a defensive position that had stopped an entire company's advance. The Eastern front in 1971 depended on speed — every hour that Indian columns were held up gave Pakistan time to regroup and possibly request international intervention. Acts of individual courage like Ekka's, repeated across dozens of engagements in Bangladesh, are what allowed the Eastern command to reach Dhaka in just 13 days. His Param Vir Chakra is a recognition that the decisive moments of great wars are often decided not by generals but by lance naiks who refuse to let their comrades die.

blocks.title: Legacy
blocks.body: Albert Ekka's story carries a meaning beyond military valour. He came from one of the most marginalised communities in India — a tribal, rural, working-class Catholic from the Chota Nagpur plateau — and he died for a country that has not always done right by communities like his. Yet he gave everything without reservation. His story challenges every citizen to ask what their own relationship to duty and nation is, and whether it is as unconditional as his was. The Albert Ekka Chowk in Ranchi, the memorial in Gumla, and the perpetuation of his name in 14 Guards ensure that the India he defended remembers him. He is a reminder that India belongs to all its people — and that all its people have always stood for it.

lessons.reflection: Albert Ekka came from one of India's smallest, most marginalised tribal communities and died fighting for the entire nation. What does this tell us about who India belongs to? And what does it demand of those who benefit from the freedom soldiers like him secured?

quiz.question: In which regiment did Lance Naik Albert Ekka serve during the 1971 war?
From which state (as constituted today) did Lance Naik Albert Ekka hail?
To which tribal community did Albert Ekka belong?
At which location in the 1971 Eastern front did Albert Ekka perform his act of supreme valour?
What gallantry award did Lance Naik Albert Ekka receive posthumously for his actions on 3 December 1971?

lessons.rank: Major (at time of battle); later promoted to Colonel
lessons.value: Held the Basantar bridgehead through multiple armoured counter-attacks — wounded but refused evacuation till ceasefire
lessons.hook: His company was outnumbered, under tank fire, and he was seriously wounded — yet he went from trench to trench, telling his men: not one step back.

blocks.title: Life Story
blocks.body:
Hoshiar Singh was born on 5 May 1936 in Sisana village of Haryana, into a family with deep roots in the farming heartland of the state. He was commissioned into the 3 Grenadiers Regiment — one of the Indian Army's oldest and most distinguished infantry regiments — and in 1963 was first posted to the North East Frontier Agency (NEFA). He participated in the 1965 war as a young officer, gaining the operational experience and hard tactical grounding that would prove decisive six years later.

On 15 December 1971, during the climactic phase of the 1971 war, 3 Grenadiers were given the task of establishing a bridgehead across the Basantar river in the Shakargarh Sector of Punjab — a strategically vital crossing that would allow Indian forces to drive deep into Pakistani territory. Major Hoshiar Singh was ordered to capture the enemy locality of Jarpal, a heavily fortified position held in strength by Pakistani infantry and armour. Under intense artillery and tank fire, Major Singh's company completed its objective, seizing and holding the bridgehead despite devastating shelling.

What followed over the next two days was even more demanding than the initial assault. The Pakistani Army, recognising the strategic danger, launched multiple counter-attacks — coordinated infantry and armoured assaults designed to push the Indians back across the Basantar and restore their defensive line. Major Hoshiar Singh, unmindful of the heavy shelling and tank fire that swept the bridgehead, went from trench to trench — encouraging his men, repositioning defenders, and personally manning a machine gun when the situation demanded it. He was seriously wounded during the fighting, but refused to be evacuated. He remained at his post, commanding and inspiring his soldiers until the ceasefire was declared.

Hoshiar Singh was awarded the Param Vir Chakra — the nation's highest gallantry award — for his absolute bravery, tactical judgement, and steadfast devotion in the line of duty. He eventually retired from the army as Colonel.

blocks.title: Impact on India
blocks.body: The Shakargarh Sector was one of the most critical axes on the Western front in the 1971 war. Pakistan's defence of the Basantar river line was designed to prevent Indian forces from outflanking the Shakargarh bulge — a Pakistani salient that would have allowed India to threaten Sialkot if breached. By establishing and holding the Jarpal bridgehead against repeated armoured counter-attacks, Major Hoshiar Singh and 3 Grenadiers denied Pakistan the ability to restore its defensive line, contributing directly to the collapse of Pakistani resistance in the sector. His refusal to be evacuated despite serious wounds — choosing to remain with his men through two days of sustained counter-attack — is a defining example of the standard of leadership the Indian Army expects of its officers.

blocks.title: Legacy
blocks.body: Colonel Hoshiar Singh is remembered at the Grenadiers Regimental Centre and across his home state of Haryana, which has produced a remarkable number of India's finest soldiers. His story is notable for the combination of qualities it illustrates: tactical competence in the initial assault, steadiness under sustained counter-attack, and the personal courage to remain wounded in position when evacuation would have been entirely justified. He was also, in his personal life, an outstanding volleyball player who represented Punjab in the nationals — a reminder that the soldiers who achieve the highest honours in war are often, in peacetime, ordinary men of varied talents and enthusiasms.

lessons.reflection: Hoshiar Singh was seriously wounded but refused evacuation — he stayed to command his men through two days of counter-attacks. What is the difference between self-sacrifice and stubbornness? How do you know, in the heat of the moment, that staying is the right choice and not simply pride?

To lead a flanking armoured column toward the Shakargarh town

modules.title: Heights of Courage — The Kargil War
modules.focus: Pakistani troops and militants occupied Indian peaks above 15,000 feet — Indian soldiers had to recapture them by climbing straight up under fire, in the world's highest battlefield.
modules.activity: Look at photographs of Tiger Hill, Tololing, or Point 5140. Write a short paragraph about what it would feel like to climb that mountain while under fire.
modules.challenge: Write a letter addressed to Captain Vikram Batra or Captain Manoj Kumar Pandey as if you were a fellow soldier. Tell them what their sacrifice means to you.
modules.summary:
In the winter of 1998–99, Pakistani soldiers and militants secretly occupied a string of commanding heights in the Kargil-Drass sector of Jammu & Kashmir — peaks ranging from 15,000 to 18,000 feet — in an operation codenamed Koh-i-Paima, calculated to sever the Srinagar-Leh highway and force India into negotiations over Kashmir. The infiltration was discovered by Indian Army patrols in May 1999, and the government launched Operation Vijay to recapture every occupied position.

The terrain presented a challenge unlike anything seen in modern warfare: near-vertical cliffs, boulders the size of buildings, zero cover, and defenders who could observe attackers from the moment they left their base camps. Indian soldiers had to climb into fortified positions while under machine-gun fire, artillery, and rocket attack, often at night, carrying weapons and ammunition at altitudes where each breath required twice the effort of sea level. Major battles unfolded across the Drass, Mushkoh, Kaksar, and Batalik sub-sectors — Tololing, Tiger Hill, Point 5140, Point 4875, and the Khalubar-Jubar complex — each requiring individual acts of extraordinary personal courage to break heavily fortified Pakistani positions. The Indian Air Force launched Operation Safed Sagar — the first significant use of air power in the high Himalayas — deploying MiG-21, MiG-27, and Mirage 2000 aircraft in ground-attack and close air support roles, suffering early losses before adapting tactics to the extreme altitude and terrain.

Sustained international pressure, particularly from the United States, combined with India's military success, forced Pakistan to order a withdrawal; Pakistani troops began pulling back in mid-July, and India declared complete victory on 26 July 1999 — a date celebrated annually as Kargil Vijay Diwas. Four soldiers were awarded the Param Vir Chakra for Kargil — two posthumously and two to living heroes — the highest number of PVCs in any single conflict since the 1971 war, a testament to the scale of individual heroism that the operation demanded.

moduleQuiz.question: What is the Indian Army's operational name for the campaign to recapture Kargil heights in 1999?
On what date is Kargil Vijay Diwas celebrated each year, marking India's declaration of complete victory?
How many Param Vir Chakras were awarded for gallantry during the Kargil War of 1999?
What famous phrase did Captain Vikram Batra radio back to headquarters immediately after capturing Point 5140?
Which peak is most directly associated with Captain Manoj Kumar Pandey's final and fatal battle in the Batalik sector?

lessons.value: Captured Point 5140, led assault on Point 4875, died pulling a wounded colleague to safety
lessons.hook: After capturing a near-impregnable peak at 17,000 feet, he grabbed the radio and declared 'Yeh Dil Maange More' — and then turned around and went back up for another.

blocks.title: Life Story
blocks.body:
Vikram Batra was born on 9 September 1974 in Palampur, a small hill town in Himachal Pradesh, to Girdhari Lal Batra, a government school principal, and Kamal Kanta Batra. He was the second of twin brothers — his identical twin, Vishal, would later join the Indian Army in his memory. Vikram was by all accounts an energetic, warm, and fearless young man: a champion table-tennis player, a college student who charmed everyone around him, and someone whose personality filled rooms. He had been accepted into a prestigious merchant navy training programme — a lucrative career path — but declined it when he caught sight of an Indian Army officer in uniform at a selection centre and felt something shift inside him. He said later that he could not imagine doing anything else.

Commissioned into the 13 JAK Rifles in 1997 after training at the Indian Military Academy, Batra was a young lieutenant when the Kargil conflict began. By the time he was assigned to recapture Point 5140 — a 17,000-foot peak in the Drass sub-sector held by well-entrenched Pakistani soldiers — he was a captain, and his men already knew him as a leader who led from the front. The assault on Point 5140 on the night of 19–20 June 1999 was conducted in darkness, up near-vertical rock faces swept by enemy fire. Batra directed his platoon's climb, engaged enemy positions at close quarters, and cleared the peak in a fierce firefight. When he radioed back to headquarters, he used the phrase 'Yeh Dil Maange More' — a Pepsi advertising slogan he had adapted with characteristic irreverence — and the words, broadcast on television, became the slogan of the entire war overnight, repeated by soldiers, journalists, and the public alike.

Assigned next to the assault on Point 4875 — an even more difficult objective — Batra led his company up another sheer face on 7 July 1999. The fighting was brutal and at close range. During the battle, a fellow officer, Lieutenant Naveen, was badly wounded in the open. Batra moved forward under direct fire to pull him back to cover. In that final act of going forward to retrieve his brother officer, he was hit by a burst of automatic fire and killed. He was 24 years old. His last words, according to those present, were 'Jai Mata Di.'

blocks.title: Impact on India
blocks.body: The fall of Point 5140 — and Batra's exuberant radio message — was a turning point in the public experience of the Kargil War. India was watching the conflict unfold on television in real time, often with anxiety and grief; Batra's voice, his youth, and his bravado gave the nation something it badly needed: a face and a voice for its soldiers' courage. His death, coming so soon after, made that image permanent. He was awarded the Param Vir Chakra posthumously — India's highest gallantry decoration. The government of Himachal Pradesh named a stretch of the Manali-Leh highway in his honour, and numerous schools, roads, and institutions across India carry his name. His parents, with extraordinary grace, accepted his death as the price their son had chosen to pay and have spent decades keeping his story alive.

blocks.title: Legacy
blocks.body: Vikram Batra's twin brother Vishal, deeply affected by his brother's death, joined the Indian Army — a decision that underscored the depth of the family's commitment and the personal weight of Vikram's sacrifice. The 2021 Bollywood film Shershaah — his code name during Kargil — brought his story to an entirely new generation of Indians and became one of the most-watched films of the year. But beyond the films and the memorials, his legacy lives in the simplest terms: he was a young man who had options, chose service, and when the moment came, chose someone else's life over his own. 'Yeh Dil Maange More' is no longer an advertisement. It is a statement about what it means to give everything and still want to give more.

lessons.reflection: Vikram Batra was pulling a wounded colleague to safety when he was killed — the last act of his life was saving someone else. How do we carry someone's story forward after they're gone? And what does it mean to choose another person's life over your own in the final moment?

quiz.question: In which regiment did Captain Vikram Batra serve during the Kargil War?
What was Captain Vikram Batra's code name during the Kargil operations?
What did Captain Batra say over the radio immediately after his company captured Point 5140?
options: Jai Hind — the peak is ours
Na Chhodnu — don't let them escape

quiz.question: What was Captain Vikram Batra doing when he was fatally shot on Point 4875 on 7 July 1999?
options: Leading the final assault on an enemy bunker

quiz.question: What gallantry award was Captain Vikram Batra given for his actions in the Kargil War?

lessons.value: Cleared four fortified enemy bunkers personally in the Batalik sector before being killed at the last
lessons.hook: He wrote in his diary that some goals are so worthy it is glorious even to fail — then went out and proved he believed every word.

blocks.title: Life Story
blocks.body:
Manoj Kumar Pandey was born on 25 June 1975 in Sitapur, a district in Uttar Pradesh, into a middle-class family. From childhood, he was fixed on a single ambition: to become an Indian Army officer. He was disciplined, studious, and deeply motivated, and those who knew him remembered that his commitment to the army was not a young man's passing fantasy but a considered, passionate vocation. He kept a diary, and in it he wrote a sentence that would become his most quoted words after his death: 'Some goals are so worthy, it is glorious even to fail.' He was commissioned in June 1997 into the 1/11 Gorkha Rifles — one of the most decorated Gorkha battalions in the Indian Army — and within two years, he was being tested in conditions none of his training could fully have prepared him for.

The Batalik sector of Kargil was the most remote and perhaps the most heavily fortified of the Pakistani-held positions. The Khalubar and Jubar hills — collectively known as Khalubar Top — were defended by dug-in machine-gun posts, rocket launchers, and well-supplied troops who held every advantage of elevation and observation. On 3 July 1999, Captain Pandey was ordered to lead his Gorkha platoon in an assault on these heights. The approach required climbing near-vertical rock faces under sustained machine-gun and rocket fire. Pandey led from the front throughout. He personally assaulted and cleared the first enemy bunker, sustaining a wound from grenade fragments. He continued, cleared the second bunker, was wounded a second time, and continued still. At the third bunker, his men were pinned — Pandey moved alone to flank the position and destroyed it. Approaching the fourth and final bunker, he turned to his men and shouted 'Na Chhodnu' — in Nepali, 'don't spare them.' Those were the last words his soldiers heard from him. He was killed clearing that final position. He was 24 years old.

Pandey was posthumously promoted to the rank of Captain and awarded the Param Vir Chakra. His actions on Khalubar that day opened the route for Indian forces to secure the entire sub-sector.

blocks.title: Impact on India
blocks.body: The Batalik sub-sector was the last major area of Pakistani occupation, and Khalubar was one of its toughest positions. Pandey's willingness to keep attacking after two wounds — alone, against fortified bunkers — exemplified the spirit that ultimately made Operation Vijay a complete Indian victory. His Param Vir Chakra citation noted that his actions 'inspired his men to heights of courage that cleared the position and secured the Batalik sector.' For the families of soldiers in Uttar Pradesh, he became a symbol that sacrifice and duty were not merely the preserve of famous regiments or famous names — that a young man from Sitapur could stand with the greatest heroes of the republic.

blocks.title: Legacy
blocks.body: Captain Manoj Kumar Pandey is honoured at the National War Memorial in New Delhi, where his name is inscribed alongside those of every Indian soldier who died in the service of the nation after 1947. A statue was erected in Sitapur; roads and schools across Uttar Pradesh carry his name. His regiment — 1/11 Gorkha Rifles — honours him at every regimental function. His diary entry — 'some goals are so worthy, it is glorious even to fail' — is quoted in military academies and schools as a statement about why we choose difficult things. He did not fail. But he showed that the willingness to risk failure for something worthy is itself a form of greatness.

lessons.reflection: Pandey wrote 'some goals are so worthy it is glorious even to fail.' He didn't fail — but what does this attitude tell us about how to approach any great challenge in life? Is there a goal in your own life that is worthy enough to pursue even if you might not succeed?

quiz.question: In which regiment did Captain Manoj Kumar Pandey serve during the Kargil War?
In which sub-sector of Kargil did Captain Pandey lead his final assault on 3 July 1999?
What did Captain Manoj Kumar Pandey write in his personal diary that became his most famous words?
options: Death is not the end — service is eternal

lessons.rank: Naib Subedar (Rifleman at time of action)
lessons.value: Shot twice, fought on — cleared enemy positions single-handed at Flat Top (Point 4875)
lessons.hook: Shot in the forearm. Kept running. Shot in the chest. Kept fighting. He is alive today, and the memory of what he did lives in his body.

blocks.title: Life Story
blocks.body:
Sanjay Kumar was born on 3 March 1976 in Bakain village, Bilaspur district, Himachal Pradesh — a small mountain community not far from Palampur, the birthplace of his regiment-mate Vikram Batra. He enlisted as a Rifleman in the 13 JAK Rifles, the same battalion in which Captain Batra served, and by July 1999 was part of the Indian Army's push to recapture the heights of the Mushkoh Valley.

On 4 July 1999, during the assault on the feature known as Area Flat Top — a component of the Point 4875 complex — Sanjay Kumar's company came under withering automatic fire from enemy positions that had pinned down the entire advance. Unable to move, the company was in danger of taking catastrophic casualties. Sanjay Kumar made a decision that no order required of him: he rose from cover and charged the enemy position alone. He was shot through the forearm almost immediately. He did not stop. Continuing his charge, he reached the first enemy bunker, killed three Pakistani soldiers in close hand-to-hand combat, and destroyed the position. He then moved to assault a second bunker. He was shot again — this time in the chest. He cleared the second bunker despite this wound, enabling his company to resume its advance. His actions broke the deadlock and were directly instrumental in India's recapture of Flat Top.

Sanjay Kumar survived both wounds and was evacuated, treated, and recovered. He was promoted over the years to the rank of Naib Subedar. He is one of only four living Param Vir Chakra recipients in India today — a distinction that makes him a living embodiment of the standard the award represents.

blocks.title: Impact on India
blocks.body: The PVC is most often discussed as a posthumous award — the majority of its recipients died in the act of earning it. Sanjay Kumar's survival makes his story distinctive: he carries in his body the permanent record of what he did. His existence as a living recipient means that the award's meaning can be communicated in person, in schools and barracks and ceremonies, by the man himself. His continued service after Kargil — rising through the ranks — demonstrated that the act of extraordinary courage was not a single isolated moment but the expression of a character that continued to serve. The army has used his testimony and presence in recruitment and training contexts to illustrate what the values of service and courage look like in practice.

blocks.title: Legacy
blocks.body: Naib Subedar Sanjay Kumar is one of the Indian Army's most tangible connections to the Kargil War — not a name on a memorial, but a man who appears in uniform at functions, speaks to young soldiers, and represents the living continuity of the PVC tradition. His home district of Bilaspur has honoured him, and his regiment maintains his citation prominently. For students and young people, he is perhaps the most direct reminder that extraordinary courage is not a property of the past — it is something that a living person, still walking among us, chose to exercise on a July morning on a Himalayan peak.

lessons.reflection: Sanjay Kumar was shot twice and kept fighting. He is alive today and carries those memories — in his body and his mind. What is the difference between reading about courage and having lived it? What does it mean to be a living witness to your own most extreme moment?

quiz.question: In which regiment did Sanjay Kumar serve when he performed his act of gallantry in the Kargil War?
At which location did Sanjay Kumar perform his gallant action on 4 July 1999?
How many times was Sanjay Kumar shot during his assault on the enemy positions at Flat Top?
options: Twice — in the forearm and in the chest
Three times — in both legs and the arm
He was not shot but was wounded by grenade fragments

quiz.question: What gallantry award did Sanjay Kumar receive for his actions during the Kargil War?
What makes Naib Subedar Sanjay Kumar's Param Vir Chakra especially notable compared to most other PVC recipients?
options: He was the youngest person ever to receive it
He received it for a peacetime act of bravery

lessons.award: Param Vir Chakra (survived — youngest-ever PVC recipient at age 19)
lessons.value: Shot three times, climbed Tiger Hill alone, destroyed three bunkers — the youngest person ever to receive the Param Vir Chakra
lessons.hook: The first assault team was wiped out. He was 19, and shot three times. He climbed the rest of the way himself.

blocks.title: Life Story
blocks.body:
Yogendra Singh Yadav was born on 10 May 1980 in Ahir village, Bulandshahr district, Uttar Pradesh. He came from a family with a strong soldiering tradition — his grandfather had served in World War II, and the army was a natural path for a young man from a community that had always given soldiers to India. He enlisted in the 18 Grenadiers and by 1999, at the age of 19, was a Grenadier — the rank equivalent of a private — with barely a year of service behind him. He was in every sense a new soldier.

On the night of 3–4 July 1999, the 18 Grenadiers were tasked with assaulting Tiger Hill — the most strategically important and most heavily fortified peak of the entire Kargil War. Tiger Hill commanded the Srinagar-Leh highway and had been the centrepiece of Pakistan's defensive plan. The assault required climbing a near-vertical 1,000-foot cliff face in darkness while Pakistani troops above hurled grenades and poured automatic-weapon fire down the slope. Yogendra Singh Yadav was part of the Ghatak platoon (an elite commando unit within the battalion) tasked with leading the climb. As his rope-climbing party neared the top, Pakistani soldiers detected them and opened fire with grenades and machine guns. The leading team was almost entirely wiped out in moments — most men were hit, some fell, the assault seemed finished before it had begun.

Yogendra Singh Yadav volunteered to continue the climb alone. He moved up the rope, was hit by grenade fragments and three separate bullet wounds — through both legs and one arm — and climbed on. Reaching the top, he engaged three enemy bunkers with grenades and close-quarters combat, destroying each in turn and clearing the path for the rest of his platoon to gain the summit. His actions were the turning point that allowed Tiger Hill to fall to Indian forces. He was taken for dead by his own unit and was placed on the casualty list — and then he walked into the aid post, alive, to the astonishment of those who had already mourned him.

Yogendra Singh Yadav was 19 years, 1 month, and 24 days old at the time of the action — the youngest person in the history of India to be awarded the Param Vir Chakra.

blocks.title: Impact on India
blocks.body: The capture of Tiger Hill on 4 July 1999 was the single most important military achievement of the Kargil War, and Yogendra Singh Yadav's solo push up the cliff face was the act that made it possible. The image of a teenager — wounded three times, carrying on alone — represented in visceral form the spirit of sacrifice that the entire nation was witnessing from afar. His survival and subsequent award of the PVC drew attention to the Grenadiers regiment and to the many junior soldiers — privates and lance corporals, not famous officers — who formed the backbone of every assault. He was a boy from a village in Uttar Pradesh, and he had done something that would have been extraordinary even for the most experienced soldier.

blocks.title: Legacy
blocks.body: Yogendra Singh Yadav remained in the Indian Army after Kargil and was promoted through the non-commissioned ranks, retiring as a Subedar Major. He is a living recipient of the Param Vir Chakra, and the youngest in the award's history. He has been a vocal presence in public life — speaking at schools, at military institutions, and in media — bringing the reality of the Kargil heights to audiences who were not alive in 1999. His grandfather's example, a generation earlier, flows through him: the tradition of ordinary men from ordinary villages who stand between India and its adversaries, not because they were born exceptional, but because they chose to be.

lessons.reflection: Yogendra was 19 years old — the same age many people start college. He had been shot three times and the assault team around him had been wiped out. He climbed the rest of the way alone. What does this tell us about what the human body and will can endure? And what does the word 'ordinary' mean after a story like this?

lessons.value: Shot down over Pakistan, held as POW for 8 days — maintained composure and gave only name, rank, and number in captivity
lessons.hook: His plane went down over enemy territory. He was alone, captured, and interrogated for 8 days. He came home with his dignity completely intact.

blocks.title: Life Story
blocks.body:
K.K. Nachiketa was born in 1969 and joined the Indian Air Force as a fighter pilot, eventually flying the MiG-27 ground-attack aircraft. The MiG-27, known in IAF service as the Bahadur (Brave), was one of the primary platforms used in Operation Safed Sagar — the IAF's contribution to Operation Vijay — conducting close air support and strike missions against Pakistani positions on the mountain peaks. Operating at extreme altitudes with limited engine performance margins, the aircraft were vulnerable to both terrain and technical failures in ways that low-altitude missions over plains would not present.

On 27 May 1999 — the earliest days of the IAF's involvement in the Kargil campaign — Nachiketa was flying a MiG-27 strike sortie over Pakistani-occupied territory when the aircraft suffered a technical malfunction. The engine flamed out, and the aircraft became uncontrollable. He ejected successfully, but his ejection seat carried him down into Pakistani-controlled territory, where he was captured by Pakistani forces within hours of landing. He was taken into Pakistani military custody and became India's first prisoner of war since the 1971 conflict.

Over the next 8 days, Nachiketa was held in Pakistani custody, subjected to interrogation, and made available to Pakistani media — his appearance on television became a significant episode in the information war that accompanied the military conflict. In all interactions with his captors and before the cameras, he maintained strict adherence to the Geneva Convention code of conduct: he gave his name, rank, and number, and nothing more. His composure, his refusal to be used as a propaganda instrument, and his quiet dignity throughout captivity were widely noted. He was repatriated to India on 3 June 1999 under diplomatic pressure, particularly from the United States. He returned to flying duties and continued his career in the IAF.

blocks.title: Impact on India
blocks.body: Nachiketa's capture and repatriation played out on Indian and international television at a moment when the Kargil conflict was being watched by the entire world. His demeanour in captivity — calm, composed, giving nothing away — stood in sharp contrast to what his captors had hoped to extract from him. The episode demonstrated that India's armed forces maintained their discipline even in the most extreme circumstances of capture and isolation. His release also marked the first significant diplomatic intervention by the United States in the conflict, signalling to the world that Pakistan's military adventure had failed on every level — military, diplomatic, and informational.

blocks.title: Legacy
blocks.body: Squadron Leader Nachiketa is awarded the Vir Chakra — India's third-highest wartime gallantry award — for his conduct during the mission and in captivity. He is a reminder that the Kargil War involved every branch of the Indian armed forces, not only the army soldiers who climbed the peaks. The IAF lost aircraft and aircrew in Operation Safed Sagar, and Nachiketa's story represents those losses and that service. He also represents a dimension of military courage that is less often discussed: not the charge up a hill, but the quiet maintenance of dignity and identity when you are alone, surrounded, and have nothing to rely on but your own character.

lessons.reflection: Nachiketa was a prisoner in an enemy country for 8 days — surrounded, alone, interrogated. He could not fight his way out or call for help. All he had was his own character. What does it mean to maintain your dignity and composure when you have nothing left to rely on but yourself? Is that a different kind of courage from what we saw in the other lessons?

modules.title: Beyond Borders — Special Ops, Peacekeeping & Earlier Wars
modules.war: Multiple — WWII, Congo 1961, Siachen 1987, Sri Lanka 1987, Mumbai 2008
modules.focus: Indian soldiers have served far beyond conventional wars — in WWII jungles, UN peacekeeping missions, the icy heights of Siachen, the streets of Colombo, and the corridors of the Taj Hotel — each time with exceptional courage.
modules.activity: India has contributed the most soldiers to UN peacekeeping missions of any country. Research one UN mission India participated in and write a short summary of India's role.
modules.challenge: The heroes in this module came from very different eras and places. Pick one and write about why their story feels most relevant to you personally today.
modules.summary:
India's military story did not begin in 1947 — more than three million Indian soldiers served under the British flag in the Second World War, making it the largest all-volunteer force in human history. In the jungles of Burma, Indian soldiers fought the Imperial Japanese Army in some of the most brutal and climatically punishing campaigns of the entire war, producing leaders of exceptional quality, among them Kodendera Subayya Thimayya, who commanded an infantry brigade in Burma, won the Distinguished Service Order, and later became Chief of Army Staff.

After independence, India became one of the world's most committed contributors to United Nations peacekeeping — a tradition that produced its own acts of extraordinary valour, nowhere more starkly than in the Congo in December 1961, when Captain Gurbachan Singh Salaria of 3/1 Gorkha Rifles charged a heavily armed Katangese gendarmerie force almost single-handed, destroyed two armoured vehicles, killed around forty enemy soldiers, and died in the act — becoming the only Indian ever to earn the Param Vir Chakra in a UN operation. In 1987, Naib Subedar Bana Singh led a small team up a near-vertical ice wall at 21,000 feet on the Siachen Glacier to storm the seemingly impregnable Quaid Post, earning the Param Vir Chakra and giving his name to the captured position. In the same year, Major Ramaswamy Parameswaran turned a deadly LTTE ambush in Sri Lanka into a counterattack, fighting on after being shot and earning a posthumous Param Vir Chakra.

The thread of special operations and counter-terrorism runs into the twenty-first century. On the night of 26 November 2008, ten Lashkar-e-Taiba terrorists launched coordinated attacks across Mumbai, killing 166 people and holding hostages inside the iconic Taj Mahal Palace Hotel. Major Sandeep Unnikrishnan of the National Security Guards led his commandos into the burning hotel, rescued several hostages, and was killed by a terrorist while ensuring the safety of his wounded colleague. His Ashok Chakra — India's highest peacetime gallantry award — recognises a form of courage every bit as great as any battlefield: the courage to run toward danger in a hotel corridor, in the dark, to save civilian strangers.

lessons.war: World War II — Burma Campaign; 1947–48 Kashmir War; Korean War (UN); UN Force in Cyprus
lessons.value: Commander in WWII Burma, Chief of Army Staff, UN commander — died in service
lessons.hook: He fought Japanese soldiers through Burmese jungles, became India's top general, and died in harness commanding a UN peacekeeping force in Cyprus — the soldier who never stopped serving.

blocks.title: Life Story
blocks.body:
Kodendera Subayya Thimayya was born on 30 March 1906 in Pollibetta, Coorg (now Kodagu), Karnataka, into a distinguished family with a tradition of public service. Known universally as 'Timmy' to his colleagues and friends, he was educated at the Prince of Wales Royal Indian Military College in Dehradun and went on to the Royal Military College, Sandhurst, in England — the same institution that trained many of the British Empire's finest officers. He was commissioned in 1926 and joined the 8th Gorkha Rifles, beginning a career that would span four decades and take him from the mountains of the North-West Frontier to the jungles of Burma to the corridors of power in New Delhi.

During the Second World War, Thimayya commanded an infantry brigade in the Burma Campaign, fighting the Imperial Japanese Army through some of the densest and most disease-ridden jungle terrain in the world. The Burma Campaign — which lasted from 1942 to 1945 — was among the longest and most gruelling land campaigns of the entire war, fought in extreme heat, monsoon rains, and the relentless pressure of a Japanese army that gave no quarter. Thimayya's leadership in the field earned him the Distinguished Service Order, a high British military decoration awarded for meritorious and distinguished service in the field. After independence, he commanded Indian forces in the Kashmir operations of 1947–48, helping to consolidate India's position in the valley.

In 1953, at the end of the Korean War, Thimayya was appointed to command the Neutral Nations Repatriation Commission, overseeing the extraordinarily delicate process of repatriating prisoners of war from both sides — a role that required not just military discipline but diplomatic skill of the highest order. He acquitted himself so well that his international reputation grew substantially. He became Chief of Army Staff of the Indian Army in 1957 and served until 1961 — presiding over a period of rapid modernisation and, ultimately, the growing civil-military tensions with Defence Minister V.K. Krishna Menon. In September 1959, frustrated by political interference, Thimayya submitted his resignation, then withdrew it at the request of Prime Minister Nehru — an episode that drew parliamentary debate and left the civil-military relationship permanently strained. After retirement, he was appointed to command the United Nations Force in Cyprus (UNFICYP) in 1964. He died there, in service, on 18 December 1965, suffering a heart attack in the field. He died as he had lived — at his post.

blocks.title: Impact on India
blocks.body: Thimayya's career bridged the colonial and independent eras of Indian military history in a way that almost no other figure did. He served under the British in conditions of genuine hardship and earned their highest respect; he then served independent India as its seniormost soldier, helping to shape the professional identity of the new Indian Army. His tenure as COAS coincided with a critical period of institution-building, when the Indian Army was transitioning from a colonial force to an independent national institution. His public confrontation with Krishna Menon — and the resignation affair — raised enduring questions about the proper relationship between civilian political authority and the professional military that Indian democracy has never fully resolved. His UN service in Korea and Cyprus established India as a serious and credible contributor to international peace operations, a reputation that has only grown in the decades since.

blocks.title: Legacy
blocks.body: Thimayya is commemorated at the Gorkha Regimental Centre and is remembered as one of the finest soldier-statesmen India has produced. His biography, 'Soldier of India', remains a key reference for understanding the early decades of independent India's military. In Kodagu, his home district, he is a figure of immense local pride — a son of the hills who led armies across three continents. His death in Cyprus while on UN duty stands as a symbol of the kind of soldier India has contributed to the world: one who never defines service by geography or by whose flag flies above the tent.

lessons.reflection: Thimayya served India before India was free, and served the world as a UN commander. He died in harness in Cyprus, thousands of miles from Karnataka. What does it mean to serve something larger than your own nation — and to keep serving even when it is thankless?

lessons.war: UN Operation in the Congo (ONUC), December 1961
lessons.value: The only Indian ever to receive the Param Vir Chakra in a United Nations operation
lessons.hook: He was 26, in a far-off African country, serving under a blue UN helmet. When armed gendarmes blocked the road, he charged them with a handful of Gorkhas and never came back.

blocks.title: Life Story
blocks.body:
Gurbachan Singh Salaria was born on 29 November 1935 in Jandiala Guru, Amritsar district, Punjab. He was commissioned into the Indian Army in 1957, joining the 3rd Battalion of the 1st Gorkha Rifles — one of the most battle-hardened Gorkha units in the Indian Army's order of battle. He was by all accounts a committed, energetic young officer who volunteered for the Indian contingent deployed to the Congo (present-day Democratic Republic of Congo) as part of the United Nations Operation in the Congo, known by its French acronym ONUC.

The UN had deployed forces to the Congo following the country's independence from Belgium in 1960, as the mineral-rich province of Katanga, led by Moïse Tshombe, had attempted to secede from the newly independent state. The secession was backed by foreign mercenaries and Belgian mining interests, and the Katangese gendarmerie — though irregular — was well-armed with armoured vehicles and automatic weapons. By December 1961, the UN was attempting to assert control and the situation had become violent.

On 5 December 1961, near the town of Elizabethville (now Lubumbashi), a UN convoy was stopped and blockaded by a Katangese gendarmerie force estimated at between 150 and 200 soldiers, equipped with armoured vehicles and heavy weapons. Captain Salaria was present with a small platoon of Gorkha soldiers. Facing a force that outnumbered his men by a ratio of perhaps ten to one, and with armoured vehicles blocking the route, Salaria made the decision to attack rather than wait. He led his small force in a direct charge on the gendarmerie position. In the assault, he personally destroyed two armoured vehicles with a rocket launcher and killed approximately forty enemy soldiers in close combat. He was shot in the neck during the engagement and died of his wound. He was twenty-six years old. He is the only Indian soldier ever to earn the Param Vir Chakra outside India's own wars — and the only one to earn it while wearing the blue helmet of the United Nations.

blocks.title: Impact on India
blocks.body: Salaria's death and posthumous PVC had a significance beyond individual heroism. India had staked enormous diplomatic capital on the principle of UN peacekeeping — Prime Minister Nehru was one of the architects of non-alignment and of the idea that new nations should contribute to collective international security rather than choosing sides in Cold War blocs. The fact that an Indian officer died in the Congo, performing an act of extraordinary valour for the United Nations, gave tangible, human expression to that abstract foreign-policy commitment. It demonstrated that India's soldiers did not merely parade in blue helmets but were prepared to fight and die under them with the same intensity they would bring to their own national wars. Salaria's Param Vir Chakra remains a symbol of this dimension of Indian military service.

blocks.title: Legacy
blocks.body: Captain Gurbachan Singh Salaria is commemorated at the 1st Gorkha Rifles Regimental Centre and at the National War Memorial in New Delhi. In Jandiala Guru, his hometown in Amritsar district, a memorial and a school carry his name. He is a unique figure in the PVC pantheon — not because his courage was greater than that of others, but because the setting of his courage was uniquely global. He died not for Indian territory, not for a disputed border, but for the principle that the international community could and should use armed force to protect peace. That is a kind of service whose implications are still being debated in the twenty-first century.

lessons.reflection: Salaria died not for India's territory but for international peace — in a far-off African country most Indians could not have found on a map. He put on a blue UN helmet and risked his life for strangers. What does it mean to serve humanity rather than a nation? Is that a greater act, a different act, or simply the same act of courage in a different uniform?

lessons.rank: Honorary Captain (was Naib Subedar at time of action)
lessons.value: One of only four living Param Vir Chakra recipients; captured Quaid Post at 21,000 feet
lessons.hook: At 21,000 feet — the world's highest battlefield — Pakistani forces held what everyone said was an impregnable peak. Naib Subedar Bana Singh decided to climb it anyway.

blocks.title: Life Story
blocks.body:
Bana Singh was born on 3 January 1949 in Kadyal village, Ramgarh tehsil, Jammu district. He enlisted in the 8 Jammu & Kashmir Light Infantry — a regiment with deep roots in the Jammu region — and by the late 1980s had risen to the rank of Naib Subedar, a senior junior commissioned officer with decades of service. He was deployed to the Siachen Glacier as part of India's continuous military presence in the world's highest and coldest battlefield, where Indian and Pakistani forces had been locked in a grinding standoff since India's Operation Meghdoot in 1984.

At an altitude of over 21,000 feet on the Siachen Glacier — higher than almost all mountains in the world outside the Himalayas — Pakistani forces had established a fortified position called Quaid Post on a commanding peak. The post had been established by Pakistani forces in early 1987 and dominated the Indian positions below. Its near-vertical ice approaches, its altitude, and its heavy defensive armament made it widely considered impregnable: the Pakistani soldiers there had, in effect, a fortress on top of the world that could only be approached up a vertical ice wall under fire. Indian planners debated the problem; the operation was assigned to Bana Singh.

On 23 June 1987, during Operation Rajiv, Naib Subedar Bana Singh led a small assault team up the near-vertical ice face in the darkness. The climb alone — at altitude, on ice, with weapons and ammunition — was a physical feat of almost incomprehensible difficulty. When the assault team reached the post at dawn, they attacked in fierce close combat, fighting hand-to-hand in the confined, frozen positions. Bana Singh personally killed several enemy soldiers. The post was captured. He brought his men home. In recognition of his extraordinary courage and leadership, he was awarded the Param Vir Chakra — and the captured post was renamed Bana Post in his permanent honour. He was subsequently promoted and eventually honoured with the rank of Honorary Captain. He is one of only four living PVC recipients in India today.

blocks.title: Impact on India
blocks.body: The capture of Quaid Post — renamed Bana Post — had significant tactical and symbolic consequences on the Siachen Glacier. Tactically, it removed a dominating Pakistani position that had threatened Indian logistics and movement on that sector of the glacier. Symbolically, it demonstrated that no position, however high or however apparently impregnable, was beyond the reach of Indian soldiers with sufficient will. The Siachen conflict is perhaps the most physically punishing military operation anywhere in the world — altitude sickness, frostbite, avalanches, and temperatures that can drop to minus fifty degrees Celsius kill more soldiers than bullets do. The fact that Bana Singh led men in close-quarters combat at 21,000 feet is a reminder that human courage has no altitude ceiling.

blocks.title: Legacy
blocks.body: Honorary Captain Bana Singh lives in Jammu and has been honoured with the Padma Bhushan, one of India's highest civilian awards, in addition to his Param Vir Chakra. He is a living testament — in the most literal sense — to what the award represents. The post he captured continues to be known as Bana Post, a permanent geographical inscription of his achievement in the atlas of the Siachen Glacier. For students and young people, he is perhaps the most visceral example of what physical and moral courage look like in combination: a soldier who trained his body to perform feats most humans cannot imagine, and then used that body to serve his country at the highest point on earth.

lessons.reflection: Bana Singh climbed a near-vertical ice face at 21,000 feet to reach enemy soldiers at the top. His body was his weapon before any gun was. At what point does physical endurance become something spiritual — something that goes beyond what the body should be able to do? Have you ever pushed through something that you thought was impossible?

lessons.war: Operation Pawan — Sri Lanka (Indian Peace Keeping Force), November 1987
lessons.value: Turned an LTTE ambush into a counterattack; killed the enemy soldier who shot him before dying of his own wounds
lessons.hook: His company walked into an ambush. Instead of taking cover, he counterattacked. Even after being shot at close range, he killed the man who shot him — and then gave his life.

blocks.title: Life Story
blocks.body:
Ramaswamy Parameswaran was born on 13 September 1946 in Bombay (now Mumbai), Maharashtra. He was commissioned into the 8 Maharashtra Regiment and distinguished himself not merely as a fighter but as a rounded and intellectually curious officer — he held a Master's degree, was an avid reader, and was well-known in his regiment for his cheerfulness, physical fitness, and the warmth he showed to his men. By 1987 he was forty-one years old, an experienced and respected major, and one of the more senior officers in the Indian Peace Keeping Force (IPKF) deployed to Sri Lanka.

India had sent the IPKF to Sri Lanka in 1987 following the Indo-Sri Lanka Accord signed by Prime Minister Rajiv Gandhi and President J.R. Jayewardene. The force's mandate was to disarm the Liberation Tigers of Tamil Eelam (LTTE) and other Tamil armed groups in northern Sri Lanka — a mission that very quickly turned into a brutal urban and jungle counterinsurgency war. The LTTE, under Velupillai Prabhakaran, had no intention of disarming and engaged the IPKF in fierce guerrilla fighting across the Jaffna peninsula and the Vanni. Indian casualties in Sri Lanka were heavy — ultimately the IPKF lost over a thousand soldiers — and the operation was politically contentious at home.

On 25 November 1987, near the village of Uduville on the Jaffna peninsula, Major Parameswaran was leading his company through an area that the LTTE had carefully prepared for ambush. The LTTE opened fire from multiple concealed positions, catching the company in a killing ground. Rather than go to ground and wait — which in an ambush can be fatal — Parameswaran immediately counter-attacked, rushing the ambush positions and directing his men in a determined assault that broke the LTTE's plan. In the ferocious close-quarters fighting that followed, he was shot at close range by an LTTE fighter. Even after being hit, he continued to fight, killed the enemy soldier who had shot him, and kept commanding and rallying his men until his wounds were no longer survivable. He died shortly thereafter. He was forty-one years old — one of the oldest and most experienced officers to be killed in action during the Sri Lanka deployment.

blocks.title: Impact on India
blocks.body: The IPKF operation in Sri Lanka remains one of the most complex, controversial, and costly military deployments in India's post-independence history. Unlike India's other wars, it was not fought on Indian soil or in defence of Indian territory — it was fought in a foreign country, against a well-organised insurgent force, under an evolving and politically fragile mandate. The soldiers who served and died in Sri Lanka did so largely without the public commemoration that the wars of 1947, 1965, or 1971 received. Parameswaran's Param Vir Chakra drew attention to the reality of what Indian soldiers were doing and dying for in Jaffna and the Vanni — not in a clean, declared war, but in the grinding, ambush-ridden reality of counterinsurgency in a foreign land. His posthumous PVC is, in one sense, the highest formal acknowledgement India gave to the sacrifices of the IPKF.

blocks.title: Legacy
blocks.body: Major Ramaswamy Parameswaran is commemorated at the Maharashtra Regiment Centre and at the National War Memorial in New Delhi. In Mumbai, a road has been named in his honour, and his regiment observes an annual memorial. He represents a generation of IPKF soldiers who served in one of India's most difficult and underacknowledged military deployments, and whose sacrifices have been slowly receiving the recognition they deserve as historical memory of the period deepens. His story — a scholar, a sportsman, a cheerful officer, killed at forty-one in someone else's civil war — reminds us that the face of military courage is often not a young recruit in his first battle, but an experienced man who knows exactly what he is risking and goes forward anyway.

lessons.reflection: Parameswaran turned an ambush — the worst kind of surprise, designed to make you a helpless victim — into a counterattack. He refused to be a victim of circumstances, even after being shot. What does it mean to refuse to accept the role of victim when circumstances force it upon you? Is that something only soldiers can do, or is it a capacity all of us have?

lessons.regiment: 7th Battalion, Bihar Regiment; National Security Guards (NSG)
lessons.war: Operation Black Tornado — 2008 Mumbai Terrorist Attacks
lessons.value: Led NSG commandos into the Taj Hotel to rescue hostages; gave his life ensuring a wounded colleague escaped
lessons.hook: When terrorists held the Taj Hotel, he led ten commandos inside — and his last act was making sure his wounded colleague got out alive.

blocks.title: Life Story
blocks.body:
Sandeep Unnikrishnan was born on 15 March 1977 in Kozhikode, Kerala, to K. Unnikrishnan and Dhanalakshmi. His father, a scientist with the Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO) in Bengaluru, raised Sandeep in a home where discipline and public service were values lived rather than preached. From an early age, Sandeep was drawn to the armed forces with a certainty that never wavered. He joined the National Defence Academy in Pune in 1995, earned his commission in 1999, and was posted as a Lieutenant to the 7th Battalion, Bihar Regiment — an infantry unit with a distinguished combat record.

He saw active service almost immediately. In 1999, he participated in Operation Vijay, fighting in the Kargil War in the mountains of Jammu & Kashmir. He went on to serve in postings across J&K and Rajasthan, gaining the operational experience that shaped his tactical instincts. His performance in the field marked him out, and he was selected for the National Security Guards — India's elite counter-terrorism force. At the NSG, he topped the fearsome 'Ghatak Course', the most physically and mentally demanding course in the Indian Army, a distinction that underscored the quality of the officer he had become. He was commissioned into the NSG's 51 Special Action Group (51 SAG) in 2006, training intensively for hostage rescue and counter-terrorism operations.

On the night of 26 November 2008, ten Lashkar-e-Taiba terrorists launched the most devastating terrorist attack in Indian history — a coordinated assault across Mumbai that struck hotels, a railway terminus, a Jewish cultural centre, and a hospital, killing 166 people. One of the primary targets was the iconic Taj Mahal Palace Hotel in Colaba, where terrorists had taken multiple hostages and were fighting from well-prepared positions across several floors. Major Sandeep Unnikrishnan was the team commander tasked with leading the NSG's rescue operation inside the hotel under Operation Black Tornado.

He led a group of ten commandos into the burning, smoke-filled building, moving floor to floor in darkness, clearing corridors and rooms under fire. In one room, they broke down a door and found a group of women held hostage — and simultaneously came under devastating automatic fire from the terrorists. In the firefight that followed, Commando Sunil Yadav was hit. Major Unnikrishnan placed himself between his wounded colleague and the enemy, engaging the terrorists to allow Yadav to be pulled to safety. He then pursued the terrorists who had retreated to an upper floor. He was shot from behind while pressing the assault and died of his wounds. He was 31 years old. His exceptional leadership and the rescue operations conducted under his command resulted in the elimination of all the terrorists at the Taj.

blocks.title: Impact on India
blocks.body:
The 26/11 attacks shook India in a way that few events had since Partition. The Taj Hotel, the Oberoi Trident, and Nariman House were not distant battlefields — they were places that millions of Indians and international visitors knew and loved. When NSG commandos rappelled onto the roof of the Taj and went in through the smoke, the nation watched on live television. Major Unnikrishnan's death — a decorated officer, a Kargil veteran, a man who had topped every course the army threw at him — made viscerally real the cost of putting down one's life for strangers.

His Ashok Chakra — India's highest peacetime gallantry award — placed his sacrifice alongside the finest acts of counter-terrorism courage in Indian military history. The award recognised not only his personal bravery but the entire spirit of the NSG's mission: to go toward danger when everyone else runs away, and to give civilians the last protection that trained courage can provide. His story also reframed the national conversation about counter-terrorism: that behind every successful operation, there are individual soldiers who absorb the risk with their own bodies.

blocks.title: Legacy
blocks.body:
Major Sandeep Unnikrishnan is one of the most beloved and mourned soldiers of his generation. A road in Bengaluru — Sandeep Unnikrishnan Road — runs through the city where he grew up. His family home in Bengaluru's HAL Layout has become a pilgrimage site; on each 26 November, citizens and soldiers alike arrive to pay their respects to his parents, who have borne their loss with extraordinary dignity and grace. His father, K. Unnikrishnan, has spoken publicly and powerfully about his son's life and about the country's obligation to remember those who protect it.

The NSG continues to honour him at its campus in Manesar, Haryana, where he is remembered as the standard against which NSG courage is measured. His story has been taught in schools, commemorated in films and documentaries, and invoked every year on the anniversary of 26/11. He is a reminder that heroes are not confined to declared wars and distant borders — that sometimes, the most extraordinary acts of courage happen inside a burning hotel, in the city where you live, defending people you have never met.

lessons.reflection: Major Unnikrishnan ran into a burning, terrorist-held hotel to rescue strangers. He was shot while making sure a wounded colleague escaped. What does this tell us about the relationship between duty and love — the love a soldier has for his comrades, and the duty a soldier feels toward civilians he has never met?

modules.title: Beyond the Course — Legends Worth Knowing
modules.war: Multiple Conflicts: 1947–48, 1971, and the Making of the Indian Army
modules.focus: Five extraordinary figures whose stories could not fit within a single themed module — a Field Marshal who shaped the army, a naval captain who went down with his ship, a Jemadar whose lone stand defied armies, and two icons who anchored India's greatest victories.
modules.activity: Choose one hero from this module and write a one-page tribute in any format — a letter, a poem, a newspaper editorial, or a speech. Focus on what lesson from their life speaks to you personally.
modules.challenge: Look up the National War Memorial in New Delhi online or, if possible, visit it. Find the names of at least two heroes from this course inscribed there. Reflect on what it means to have a name on a wall instead of a life.
modules.summary:
This optional bonus module gathers five stories that deserve to stand alongside the heroes of the main course — stories that span the entire arc of independent India's military history, from the Jemadar who held a post alone through a winter night in 1948, to the captain who chose to go down with his ship in the Arabian Sea in 1971.

They represent different services, different eras, and different kinds of heroism. Field Marshal K.M. Cariappa built the Indian Army from the ground up, becoming its first Indian Commander-in-Chief and setting the institutional standards that would guide it through the conflicts that followed. Brigadier Mohammad Usman made a choice at Partition that defined who he was — and then gave his life defending that choice at Nowshera. Jemadar Nand Singh of 1/11 Sikh Regiment performed a feat of individual courage in a snowbound mountain battle of World War II that has never been surpassed in the annals of Indian arms. Captain Mahendra Nath Mulla of the Indian Navy became the first naval recipient of the Maha Vir Chakra when he stayed on the bridge of his sinking ship, INS Khukri, ensuring the survival of as many of his crew as possible. And Brigadier Kuldip Singh Chandpuri held the Longewala post with 120 men against 65 tanks and 2,000 soldiers — refusing, simply, to withdraw.

These are not supplementary stories. They are essential ones. Together they form a portrait of a military tradition that, across every service and every era, has produced soldiers of extraordinary moral and physical courage.
