lessons.regiment: Rajput Regiment; later Indian Army Chief
lessons.award: Order of the Chief Commander, Legion of Merit (USA); Field Marshal (1986)
lessons.value: First Indian Commander-in-Chief of the Indian Army — the man who built the institution
lessons.hook: When India became free, it needed someone to take the army from British hands and make it Indian. One man stepped forward and shaped everything that followed.

blocks.title: Life Story
blocks.body:
Kodandera Madappa Cariappa was born on 28 January 1899 in Coorg (today Kodagu), Karnataka, into a family of the Kodava community — a people with a martial tradition stretching back centuries. He was among the first Indians selected for the batch of King's Commissioned Indian Officers at Daly Cadet College, Indore, receiving his permanent commission in 1922 and being transferred to Mesopotamia (present-day Iraq). On his return to India he was posted to the Rajput Regiment, which would remain the regimental home closest to his professional identity.

Career milestone followed career milestone: in 1933, Cariappa became the first Indian officer to complete the prestigious Staff College course at Quetta. In 1942, during World War II, he became the first Indian to be given command of a unit — a watershed moment in an army still largely commanded by British officers. He saw action in Iraq, Syria, Iran, and Burma. In 1947, he became the first Indian officer selected for the Imperial Defence College course at Camberley, UK — the highest professional development appointment in the British military world.

At Partition in August 1947, Cariappa was given the delicate and immensely important task of overseeing the division of the British Indian Army's assets and personnel between India and Pakistan — a role requiring both administrative precision and political sensitivity. When the 1947–48 Kashmir War broke out, he served as Eastern Army Commander and then General Officer Commanding-in-Chief of Western Command, personally leading operations that recaptured Zojila, Drass, and Kargil — some of the highest-altitude military operations ever conducted.

On 15 January 1949, General Cariappa became the first Indian Commander-in-Chief of the Indian Army, taking over from General Sir Francis Butcher. He retired in 1953 and went on to serve as India's High Commissioner to Australia and New Zealand. The Government of India conferred the five-star rank of Field Marshal on Cariappa in 1986, one of only two Indians ever to hold that rank. He died on 15 May 1993 at the age of 94, having outlived most of the generation he had served with and led.

blocks.title: Impact on India
blocks.body: K.M. Cariappa's impact on the Indian Army is foundational in the most literal sense. He inherited an army whose officer corps was transitioning from British to Indian command — with all the institutional uncertainty, professional adjustment, and political complexity that entailed — and he set the standards, the culture, and the professional ethos that the army carried forward. His insistence on discipline, apolitical professionalism, and meritocracy shaped the officer culture that produced the leaders of 1962, 1965, and 1971. The American President Harry S. Truman awarded him the Order of the Chief Commander of the Legion of Merit in recognition of his contribution to Allied cooperation during and after World War II. That 15 January 1949 — the day he became Commander-in-Chief — is celebrated as Army Day in India is the most fitting of all tributes: a whole nation marking the day one man took command.

blocks.title: Legacy
blocks.body: Field Marshal Cariappa's name is perpetuated across India's military institutions. The Cariappa College at the Indian Military Academy, training grounds, and several military schools carry his name. He is one of only two Indian officers to hold the five-star rank of Field Marshal — the other being Sam Manekshaw. A man of immense personal discipline and dignity, he was known for his insistence on high standards, his famous opposition to communalism in the army, and his personal accessibility to soldiers of all ranks. A lesser-known story: his own son, Flight Lieutenant K.C. 'Nanda' Cariappa, was shot down and captured by Pakistan during the 1965 war. When Pakistan offered to release Nanda as a gesture of goodwill toward his father, Field Marshal Cariappa wrote to General Ayub Khan refusing the offer — asking that his son be treated no differently from any other prisoner of war. It was perhaps the finest summary of the man.

lessons.reflection: When Cariappa's son was held as a prisoner of war in Pakistan, Cariappa refused special treatment — he asked that his son be treated like any other POW. What does this tell us about the relationship between personal loyalty and the duties of public service? Is it possible to be a great public servant and a devoted parent at the same time?

lessons.rank: Jemadar (then Naik at time of action)
lessons.award: Victoria Cross (World War II); Indian Order of Merit
lessons.value: Charged a steep ridge alone four times, wiping out three machine-gun posts — one of the supreme individual acts of gallantry in Indian military history
lessons.hook: The ridge was steep, the enemy above him, and machine guns covering every approach. He charged it four times.

blocks.title: Life Story
blocks.body:
Nand Singh was born in 1914 in Shahpur district of Punjab (in present-day Pakistan) into a Sikh family with a tradition of soldiering. He enlisted in the 1/11 Sikh Regiment of the British Indian Army and by 1944 had served with the regiment through several theatres, rising to the rank of Naik. In March 1944, his battalion was involved in operations in Burma — the most difficult and demanding theatre of World War II for Indian troops, with fighting in dense jungle, mountainous terrain, and extreme tropical conditions against a determined and experienced Japanese enemy.

On 11-12 March 1944, near Maungdaw in Burma, Nand Singh's platoon was ordered to capture a strongly held enemy position on the top of a steep ridge. The ridge was defended by Japanese soldiers in well-constructed bunkers, with machine guns covering the approaches. Any assault up the slope was under devastating fire from above. The leading section of the platoon was pinned down immediately, unable to advance or withdraw without taking catastrophic casualties.

Nand Singh, without orders and without hesitation, charged the ridge alone. He reached the top despite being wounded — and destroyed the first machine-gun post with grenades and his bayonet. He was wounded again. He charged again — and wiped out the second post. Wounded a third time, bleeding heavily, he continued. He charged the ridge a third and fourth time, destroying a third machine-gun post. By the time the position was taken, he had been wounded multiple times and had personally accounted for the entire defensive position that had stopped his platoon. The ridge was taken.

Nand Singh was awarded the Victoria Cross — the highest gallantry award in the British Empire — for this action. He was one of the last Indian soldiers to receive the VC before Independence, and one of the most decorated non-commissioned officers in the history of the Indian subcontinent.

blocks.title: Impact on India
blocks.body: The battle at Maungdaw, and the individual actions of soldiers like Nand Singh, were part of the broader campaign that eventually liberated Burma from Japanese occupation — a campaign in which Indian soldiers played by far the dominant role, accounting for the majority of Allied troops in the Southeast Asian theatre. Nand Singh's feat is remarkable even within the extraordinary annals of World War II: charging a defended ridge four times, sustaining multiple wounds, and personally destroying three machine-gun posts is an act of individual aggression and stamina that few soldiers in any army in any war have matched. His Victoria Cross was the recognition — by the highest authority then available — that his bravery was in a class by itself.

blocks.title: Legacy
blocks.body: Jemadar Nand Singh's story is less well known to general Indian audiences than those of the Param Vir Chakra recipients, partly because his defining action took place in World War II — before India's independence — and partly because the Burma Campaign itself has been somewhat underrepresented in popular Indian memory of the war. Within the Sikh Regiment, however, he is honoured as one of the regiment's greatest soldiers. His Victoria Cross is a permanent reminder that Indian soldiers fought — and fought magnificently — in every major theatre of World War II, long before India had its own gallantry award system. He lived until 1986, giving the Indian Army four decades of a living bearer of the Victoria Cross.

lessons.reflection: Nand Singh was already wounded when he charged the ridge the second time. And the third. And the fourth. At what point does courage become something beyond what we normally understand by the word? What does it tell us about the human capacity to override the most basic instincts of self-preservation?

quiz.question: In which regiment did Naik (later Jemadar) Nand Singh serve when he performed his extraordinary act of gallantry in March 1944?
In which country and campaign did Nand Singh earn the Victoria Cross in 1944?
options: North Africa — the Western Desert Campaign
Burma — the Burma Campaign against Japan

quiz.question: How many machine-gun posts did Nand Singh personally destroy in his lone charges up the ridge near Maungdaw?
What gallantry award did Naik Nand Singh receive for his actions in Burma in March 1944?
Why is Nand Singh's story somewhat less well known to general Indian audiences compared to post-Independence PVC recipients?
options: Because his action was classified as secret by the British government
Because his regiment has not promoted his story in popular media
Because his defining action took place in World War II before Indian Independence, in the relatively lesser-known Burma Campaign
Because the Victoria Cross was not considered equivalent to the Param Vir Chakra under Indian law

lessons.value: The Lion of Nowshera — chose India over a Pakistan Army commission
lessons.hook: Pakistan offered him a general's commission. He said no — and then spent his life defending the country he had chosen.

blocks.title: Life Story
blocks.body:
Mohammad Usman was born on 18 July 1912 in Bibipur village in Mau district, United Provinces (present-day Uttar Pradesh), into a Muslim family. He was commissioned into the British Indian Army in 1934 from the Indian Military Academy at Dehradun, and by the time of Independence he had established a reputation as one of the finest infantry officers of his generation — disciplined, tactically astute, deeply respected by both his officers and his men. When Partition came in 1947 and the British Indian Army was divided between the new nations of India and Pakistan, Usman faced a choice that defined the rest of his short life: he was offered a senior commission in the newly formed Pakistani Army, with the prospect of immediate promotion, on account of his religion. He declined without hesitation. India was his country, he stated plainly, and he would serve no other.

In early 1948, as Pakistani-backed tribal forces seized the town of Jhangar in the Jammu sector and then pressed toward Nowshera, Brigadier Usman was given command of the brigade tasked with holding and recovering these positions. When Nowshera came under sustained siege — completely surrounded at one point, with communications stretched and supplies dwindling — it was Usman's personal leadership, his ability to inspire troops in hopeless-seeming conditions, and his tactical judgement that held the garrison together. He organised a successful breakout of the siege, relieved the pressure on Nowshera, and then went on the offensive, personally leading the operations that recovered Jhangar from enemy hands. His soldiers called him 'The Lion of Nowshera' — a name that spread across the entire front.

On 3 July 1948, while inspecting forward positions near Naushera, Brigadier Usman was struck by an enemy artillery shell and killed instantly. He was 35 years old. In his final months, he had reportedly told colleagues that he would not survive the war — and that he did not wish to. What mattered to him, he said, was that his men would know he stood with them to the end. He was given a state funeral and buried with full military honours at the Jamia Millia Islamia ground in Delhi, where tens of thousands of citizens came to pay their respects.

blocks.title: Impact on India
blocks.body: Mohammad Usman's impact on the 1947–48 war was both tactical and profoundly symbolic. Tactically, the defence and recovery of Nowshera and Jhangar denied the raiders a springboard from which they could have threatened Jammu itself — the winter capital of the state of Jammu & Kashmir and the gateway to the plains of northern India. Had Nowshera fallen permanently, the consequences for the entire Indian position in the Jammu sector could have been catastrophic. Symbolically, his story — of a Muslim officer who chose India, fought for India, and died for India — was of enormous importance in the fraught communal atmosphere of post-Partition India. He became a powerful embodiment of the idea that the Indian Army, and India itself, was a nation defined not by religion but by shared values and citizenship. He was posthumously awarded the Maha Vir Chakra, India's second-highest wartime gallantry award.

blocks.title: Legacy
blocks.body: Brigadier Mohammad Usman is remembered as one of the most important figures of the 1947–48 Kashmir War, and his story is taught in Indian military academies as an example of both tactical excellence and moral clarity. His grave in Delhi is maintained with great care, and on the anniversaries of his death, soldiers and civilians alike come to pay their respects. A road in Delhi bears his name. His decision to reject the Pakistani commission resonates across generations as a statement about identity, loyalty, and what it means to be Indian — not as a matter of birth or religion, but as a matter of deliberate, courageous choice.

lessons.reflection: When Partition divided the subcontinent, Pakistan offered Usman a prestigious commission — he chose India instead, and gave his life for that choice. What does his decision tell us about the difference between loyalty to a religious identity and loyalty to a national one? What does it mean to choose your country?

lessons.value: First Indian Navy recipient of the Maha Vir Chakra — went down with his ship ensuring his crew could escape
lessons.hook: His ship was sinking, the crew was abandoning vessel — and he stayed on the bridge until the sea took him.

blocks.title: Life Story
blocks.body:
Mahendra Nath Mulla was born on 15 May 1926 in Gorakhpur, Uttar Pradesh. He was commissioned into the Indian Navy on 1 May 1948 — just months after Indian independence — and over the next two decades rose through the ranks, holding a variety of important appointments that reflected his ability and professional standing. By December 1971, he held the rank of Captain and had been given command of INS Khukri, a Type 14 frigate of the Western Fleet. The Khukri was one of the Indian Navy's workhorses — a versatile, capable ship central to fleet operations.

The 1971 war placed the Indian Navy in active combat for the first time since independence. On the Western seaboard, the Pakistani Navy's most formidable asset was PNS Hangor — a French-built Daphné-class submarine commanded by Commander Ahmad Tasnim. On the evening of 9 December 1971, INS Khukri and its sister ship INS Kirpan were conducting anti-submarine operations off the coast of Diu in the Arabian Sea. Despite their search, the Hangor evaded detection. At approximately 20:57, PNS Hangor fired two torpedoes at INS Khukri. Both hit. The resulting explosions were catastrophic — INS Khukri sank in approximately three minutes, taking 18 officers and 176 sailors with her.

In those three minutes, Captain Mulla displayed the qualities that define a commanding officer of the highest order. He remained on the bridge, calm and composed, directing the emergency, organising the abandonment of the vessel, and ensuring that as many of his crew as possible could escape. He did not abandon his ship. He went down with her. Of the 375 crew, 66 officers and men survived. Captain Mulla's conduct — his refusal to prioritise his own survival over his duty to his men and his ship — was recognised by the posthumous award of the Maha Vir Chakra, making him the first officer of the Indian Navy to receive it.

blocks.title: Impact on India
blocks.body: The sinking of INS Khukri was the most significant naval loss India suffered in the 1971 war — indeed, the most significant combat loss any Indian Navy vessel has experienced since independence. In the context of the war, it was a tactical Pakistani success: PNS Hangor had demonstrated the ability to operate effectively in waters defended by Indian anti-submarine units, and INS Khukri's loss removed a capable surface asset from the Western Fleet. Yet the manner in which Captain Mulla commanded his ship in its final minutes — staying at his post, keeping his composure, working to save as many lives as possible — became an enduring standard within the Indian Navy for what is expected of a commanding officer when everything goes wrong. His legacy lifted the morale of the entire Navy and armed forces in an otherwise devastating moment.

blocks.title: Legacy
blocks.body: Captain Mahendra Nath Mulla is honoured in the Indian Navy as the embodiment of the commanding officer's ultimate responsibility: a ship's captain stays with his vessel. His Maha Vir Chakra is the first such award in the Navy's history, and it is commemorated every year on 9 December — the anniversary of the sinking of INS Khukri — as the Indian Navy observes a memorial for those who were lost. A memorial to INS Khukri and her crew stands at Diu, near the site of the sinking. Captain Mulla's cool, calm, and resolute composure in the final three minutes of his life continues to inspire generations of Indian naval officers as the definitive standard of leadership under the most extreme conditions.

lessons.reflection: Captain Mulla had three minutes from the first torpedo strike to the ship sinking. In those three minutes, he chose to stay. What does it mean to hold to a standard of duty even when every instinct says save yourself? Can you think of a moment — in any context, at any scale — when you chose duty over safety or convenience?

quiz.question: Which Indian Navy vessel did Captain Mahendra Nath Mulla command when it was sunk on 9 December 1971?
Which Pakistani submarine fired the torpedoes that sank INS Khukri in the 1971 war?
What gallantry award did Captain Mahendra Nath Mulla receive posthumously, and what historic distinction did this carry for the Indian Navy?
options: Param Vir Chakra — the first ever awarded to an Indian Navy officer
Maha Vir Chakra — the first ever awarded to an Indian Navy officer
Vir Chakra — the first ever awarded posthumously in the Indian Navy
Ashok Chakra — the first peacetime gallantry award given to the Navy

quiz.question: Where does a memorial to INS Khukri and her crew stand, near the site of the ship's sinking in 1971?

lessons.rank: Major (at time of battle); later promoted to Brigadier
lessons.value: Held Longewala — 120 men against 2,000 soldiers and 65 tanks
lessons.hook: His post had 120 men, one anti-tank gun, and no reinforcements coming. The enemy had 65 tanks and 2,000 soldiers. He chose to hold.

blocks.title: Life Story
blocks.body:
Kuldip Singh Chandpuri was born on 22 November 1940 in Lyallpur — now Faisalabad, Pakistan — and came to India as a young child at Partition. Commissioned into the Indian Army, he rose to command a company of the 23 Punjab Regiment by 1971. He was a major when the war began.

On the night of 4–5 December 1971, a massive Pakistani armoured column crossed the border into the Thar Desert of Rajasthan and advanced on the small border post at Longewala. The column comprised approximately 65 tanks — mostly US-supplied M47 and M48 Pattons — and around 2,000 infantry soldiers, with a combined arms force designed to punch deep into Rajasthan and threaten Jaisalmer. The Longewala post had one company of 23 Punjab: about 120 men and a single American-made 106 mm recoilless rifle used as an anti-tank gun.

When the advancing column was detected, Chandpuri had two options: request air support and hold until dawn, or withdraw his company to avoid annihilation. His commanding officer told him he could withdraw if he judged the position untenable. Chandpuri refused to withdraw. He spread his men around the perimeter, ordered his one anti-tank gun into position, and called for IAF support. Through the long desert night, his company held the post — the recoilless rifle destroying tank after tank that tried to breach the berm, while Chandpuri kept his men calm and in position.

At dawn, the Indian Air Force arrived. HAL HF-24 Marut and Hawker Hunter jets of the Western Air Command descended on the Pakistani column, which was now stalled, exposed in open desert, and unable to manoeuvre. Thirty-six Pakistani tanks were destroyed. Over 200 Pakistani soldiers were killed. The column retreated in disorder. India's losses at Longewala: two soldiers. The battle was over.

Chandpuri was awarded the Maha Vir Chakra. He later rose to the rank of Brigadier before retiring. He died on 17 November 2018, just days before what would have been his 78th birthday.

blocks.title: Impact on India
blocks.body: The Battle of Longewala had strategic importance beyond its modest scale. Pakistan's plan in the Rajasthan sector was to use armoured columns to penetrate deep into Indian territory — to create facts on the ground that could offset the disaster unfolding in the east. Had the column broken through Longewala, it would have advanced on Jaisalmer and destabilised India's position on the Western front at a crucial moment. Chandpuri's decision to hold, and the IAF's ability to respond at dawn, turned what was designed to be a breakthrough into a rout. The battle became the defining symbol of the 1971 war on the Western front: that small, determined forces, properly supported and properly led, can defeat overwhelmingly superior enemies.

blocks.title: Legacy
blocks.body: The Battle of Longewala was immortalised in J.P. Dutta's 1997 Bollywood epic 'Border', starring Sunny Deol as Major Chandpuri, Jackie Shroff, and Akshaye Khanna. It remains one of the highest-grossing war films in Indian cinema history and has introduced the story of Longewala to multiple generations. A war memorial now stands at Longewala, where some of the destroyed Pakistani tanks remain on display — a permanent, rusting reminder of the night 120 men held the desert. Brigadier Chandpuri himself attended many commemorations and screenings over the years, always insisting that the real heroes were his men, not him. He retired a modest, quietly proud soldier, and died in 2018 knowing the country remembered.

lessons.reflection: Chandpuri held Longewala with 120 men against 2,000 soldiers and 65 tanks — and chose not to withdraw when he was given the option. What is the difference between stubbornness and steadfast resolve? How do you know, in the moment, which one you are exercising?
